nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reefs, coastal lagoons, estuarine and protected coastal waters; has been documented in dense aggregations under ledges and in caves

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet in captivity; has been documented consuming small crustaceans, algae, and frozen/freeze-dried foods. Algae-based frozen foods are recommended alongside vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp and brine shrimp.

avoidWith: large predators that may consume small schooling fish

imageCandidates.notes: Richard Ling; 768x992. Dense wild school of many individuals showing the diagnostic deep yellow body with diagonal black stripes - ideal for this schooling species; sharp and vibrant.
Belinda Forbes; 1300x1508. Single in-situ fish with vibrant natural yellow body and crisp diagonal black stripes; slightly soft from water haze but correctly ID'd and well framed.
Totti; 3437x2030. Clean aquarium side profile showing the compressed deep body shape and full stripe pattern; reasonably sharp, whole fish in frame.

summary: The Australian Stripey is a deep-bodied, boldly patterned schooling fish from the Indo-Pacific, recognized by its yellow body with diagonal black stripes. Generally considered peaceful, it is not regarded as reef-safe and is best kept in groups of three or more.
careNotes: The Australian Stripey (Microcanthus strigatus) belongs to the family Microcanthidae, a group sometimes informally called stripeys. Older hobby sources occasionally list the species under the family Kyphosidae, but that placement is now outdated; the current accepted classification puts it within family Microcanthidae under the order Centrarchiformes. In the wild, the species tends to form dense aggregations beneath ledges and inside caves on rocky and coral reefs. In the aquarium, it is widely considered a schooling fish that does best when kept in a group of three or more — a single specimen is often reported to become withdrawn or stressed. The Australian Stripey has a distinctly compressed, deep body profile that can lead to it being mistaken for a butterflyfish (family Chaetodontidae) in the trade, though the two groups are unrelated.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperature range, taxonomy, distribution, and habitat. FishBase classifies the species under family Microcanthidae (not Kyphosidae as in older hobby sources) within order Centrarchiformes. Temperature preferred range 17.5–26.4°C (wild data); max size 16.2 cm FL.
